data.title: JEPQ:CA: A Strong Alternative For Canadian Income Investors
data.description:
For Canadian investors, the buy/write strategy environment actually looks more evolved than the American counterparts. Several strategies in Canada use leverage in addition to partial option writing (i.e., options are written on a fraction of the notional portfolio value rather than the full portfolio). This often leads to better chances of capturing upsides and growing investments that can fund the payouts. I had seen this for several funds I have reviewed so far - HHIS:CA (Cautious Buy), QQCL:CA (Buy), QQCC:CA (Strong Buy).

The reason I like the combination of leverage and partial option writing is that targeting aggressive option writing is often counterintuitively futile - such strategies often do not yield the expected premium alpha even in flat markets. What really and actually works is a bull thesis in the underlying, even if in the very long term. Which is usually a broad market index underlying such as the NDX or the S&P 500 in the case of the US markets or the S&P/TSX 60 as the Canadian counterpart.

In the American markets, JEPQ is one of the rarer active buy/write strategies that tries to generate alpha through a balance of capital appreciation of the underlying portfolio (actively curated), dividends of the underlying stocks (less so as focus is on growth), and balanced option writing on partial portfolio notional. The same strategy is implemented in the Canadian markets in the form of the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income Active ETF (TSX:JEPQ:CA). For Canadian investors, JEPQ:CA is an alternative to other broad market buy/writes - QQCC:CA, QQCL:CA (US market underlying), and even the Global X Canadian S&P/TSX 60 Covered Call ETF (CNCC:CA) (local market index as the underlying).

JEPQ:CA Against QQCC And QQCL

QQCC:CA and QQCL:CA are based on the QYLD but with structural differences that make a huge difference. QQCC:CA writes options on part of the portfolio and captures upsides far better than QYLD. QQCL:CA simply adds moderate leverage to the mix and amplifies the risk-reward - a different product really than JEPQ:CA or QQCC:CA requiring patient investors with higher risk appetite.

QQCC:CA does slightly better than JEPQ:CA in terms of empirical performance. However, the outperformance is not consistent. It has clearly captured the rebound from the April lows far better, while the total returns have been quite close to each other in the late 2024-early 2025 rally and the subsequent tariff crash. Overall, if QQCC:CA is a Strong Buy, JEPQ:CA is definitely a Buy - there could be regimes where it may outdo QQCC:CA too as the differences seen are because of execution variations rather than any structural differences.

The Local Index

Since income investors should ideally be agnostic to the underlying while focusing on income, I have included an call writing strategy based on a less tech focused local index - XIU:CA, which is based on the S&P/TSX 60. Including this comparison shows the entire spectrum of risk-reward characteristics for buy/write strategies.

XIU:CA, has been less volatile in the past 5 years than the US headline indices. Lower drawdowns were clearly observed during both occasions when the US markets were volatile. In 2022-23, the Canadian markets were going through more of a time correction over a prolonged period. During the tariff crash of April 2025, too, XIU does not suffer as big a drawdown as either the S&P 500 (proxied below as SPY) or the NDX (proxied as QQQ).

CNCC:CA is based on the S&P/TSX 60, a far less volatile underlying. Generally, a less volatile underlying might mean a couple of things. It may mean bad news for a strategy that has lower option premiums to harvest. And two, the growth prospects in the long term are lower. The first perception is not necessarily true, as I have discussed here.

A higher volatility underlying like MARA often ends up gaining almost fully from the total returns of the underlying-the option gains are difficult to lock in over time because of the underlying's volatile moves.

A certain amount of optimal volatility helps, but a balance is more conducive. More importantly, the eventual determinant of a good underlying is its long-term bullish thesis as much as a less volatile growth profile. The long-term bullish thesis, fund payouts, and a less volatile underlying make the strategy more market agnostic - an easier ride for long-term investors.

The second perception against the S&P/TSX 60 as an underlying is that its long-term growth is far behind the NDX's or even the S&P 500's. The US' secular tech edge does mean a lot of room for growth over the next couple of decades at least, particularly in the era of secular AI tailwinds. However, the gap is not as wide, as seen in the past 5 years' returns. A couple of percentage points on a CAGR basis for a drawdown that is less severe by 5-10 percentage points is a balanced deal.

And here is how the balanced deal, as an underlying, works out against the US counterpart. CNCC:CA's total returns are lower by ~5 percentage points over the past year. However, look at the drawdown in JEPQ:CA to understand the interim risks you need to sign up for to harvest the superior returns in supportive regimes. I see the drawdown in JEPQ:CA at least 10 percentage points higher than in the case of CNCC:CA. That's significant volatility to handle, and if markets turn more adverse or remain under pressure for longer, JEPQ:CA could be a tougher proposition to hold on to compared to CNCC:CA.

Spoilt For Choices

While JEPQ is a straightforward winner against QYLD in the US markets, QQCC:CA, with a major structural tweak, actually becomes as good as or better than JEPQ:CA in the Canadian markets. JEPQ:CA is a buy for investors who believe in the long-term bull thesis of the NDX and can be comfortable with moderate volatility. I would keep an overweight on QQCC:CA, but JEPQ:CA also gets a look into my income portfolio. The yield is slightly lower (~9.5% TTM vs. ~10.5% for QQCC:CA), but performances will fluctuate, and we may see JEPQ:CA outrun QQCC:CA in certain markets.

For a more aggressive investor, we have QQCL:CA with leverage to amplify long-term income. On the other hand, if investors are looking for a less volatile ride, then CNCC:CA is a viable route. The total returns may be lower than JEPQ:CA, but the difference in drawdowns is stark.

data.title: Despite Challenges, Darling Ingredients Remains Tasty
data.description:
October 23rd was a fantastic day for shareholders of Darling Ingredients (NYSE:DAR). Shares of the company shot up 11.4% for the day. This came after management announced financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2025 by fiscal year. Revenue and earnings per share both came in higher than what analysts anticipated. And this, interestingly enough, convinced market participants to ignore a rather sizable downward revision in profitability for the 2025 fiscal year because of certain headwinds the company is faced with and a change to how it's approaching relaying expectations. This is the beauty of value investing. Back in May of this year, I talked about the major transformation the business was going through. I acknowledged that while financial performance was volatile, the company was cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Even during tough times, shares can perform well. And today was proof of that.

Moving forward, I expect continued volatility. After all, this is not the kind of company that will see stable financial performance. But when you consider how cheap shares are, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises, it's difficult not to be bullish. At the end of the day, I think that the company deserves enough upside to justify the 'buy' rating I had on it earlier this year.

Shares remain cheap after this tasty run

All things considered, I would say that the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a really positive time for shareholders of Darling Ingredients. The company reported revenue of $1.56 billion. That was an increase of 10% compared to the $1.42 billion the company reported at the same time last year. This growth in revenue is driven by an expansion across all three of the firm's operating segments. But before I get into that, I do think it is necessary to point out that these sales reported by the company ended up being a solid $62 million greater than what analysts were expecting to see.

The slowest-growing of the firm's operating segments was its Food Ingredients segment. This part of the company reported sales during the quarter of $380.6 million. That happened to be 6.5% above the $357.3 million reported the previous year. Management acknowledged volume softness late in the quarter because of tariffs. But that didn't stop overall sales from growing because of higher demand for collagen. And even though tariffs remain a pressing and perhaps even growing issue, management expects that future months should see greater growth because of higher demand for collagen on a global scale. On top of this, this segment reported a nice surge in profitability, with segment profits skyrocketing from $57 million to $71.6 million. Management attributed this to cost management, growth in certain areas, and the demand environment. All in all, I have no complaints here.

Even more impressive was the largest of the firm's operating segments, which is the Feed Ingredients segment. This part of the company saw revenue shoot up from $927.5 million to $1.03 billion. Management attributed this to domestic policy in the US that boosted demand for domestic fats. A pro-energy stance by the current administration, as well as tariffs, seemed to be contributors here. But in addition to this, other markets fared well also. The global rendering business for the company achieved growth in Brazil, Canada, and Europe, plus the business also saw its export protein offerings show signs of an early recovery, with demand rising and pricing trends improving. Profitability for this segment improved as well, with segment EBITDA rising from $132.2 million to $174 million.

Lastly, we have the Fuel Ingredients unit. This is the smallest of the segments, with revenue of only $154.3 million. But that was still an astounding 12.5% above the $137.1 million the company reported a year earlier. This unit did experience lower volumes that negatively affected results during the quarter. However, management also announced the closing of the sale of $125 million of production tax credits during the quarter, with plans to sell another $125 million to $175 million worth by the end of this year. This is the only segment that saw a decline in profitability, with a drop from $59.7 million to $21.6 million. As disappointing as this is, it is important to note that it was impacted by a $45.8 million hit caused by its equity ownership interest in Diamond Green Diesel. So while this is still a very real loss, at least it's not a core operational issue that the company is exhibiting.

On the bottom line, the picture is a bit complicated because management has not revealed operating cash flow figures. Those will come out when the quarterly filing is made available. But we do understand what happened with profitability as a whole. Earnings per share grew from $0.11 last year to $0.12 this year, pushing net profits up from $16.9 million to $19.4 million. In fact, earnings per share ended up being $0.11 greater than what analysts were anticipating. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $236.7 million to $244.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue is up, but profits are still lower on a year over year basis. Speaking of profits, the picture for the company does now look worse than it did previously. Before, management anticipated EBITDA of between $1.05 billion and $1.10 billion. But now, they have lowered that substantially to between $875 million and $900 million. Management stressed that this decline, however, was driven in part by the firm's shift to focus only on its core ingredients business, essentially removing its DGD operation from the mix. However, they specified that headwinds also remain, with public policy volatility proving to be particularly problematic. This does not mean that the company won't achieve a higher level of EBITDA. It just means that the company is not reporting estimates for the renewable operations that it has in its DGD joint venture that is 50% owned by it and 50% by Valero Corporation (VLO) because not even they are confident in guiding what those results might look like given the current environment.

This might seem controversial. But to get a picture of the core business, I think that valuing the company based on the assumption that DGD does not even exist is the way to go here. Even with that, the picture for the company looks solid. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued based on estimates that I figured for the 2025 fiscal year. I also looked at the company through the lens of results from last year. These are multiples that, to me at least, are quite appealing. And relative to some other similar firms, shares are attractive also. In the table below, you can see how, on both a price-to-operating cash flow basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, three of the five firms that I stacked it up against are more expensive than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Darling Ingredients 8.2 10.7
Ingredion (INGR) 6.7 6.5
Fresh Del Monte Produce (FDP) 8.4 7.7
Village Farms International (VFF) 17.5 47.4
Limoneira (LMNR) 9.7 77.9
Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) 5.4 15.7

Takeaway

All things considered, I must say that I am relatively bullish about Darling Ingredients. Management's decision to change how it approaches guiding results seems necessary and appropriate. And by focusing on the company in this manner as well, we can get a good idea as to the overall health of the core operations. Shares look cheap even with that factored in. This is true not only on an absolute basis but also relative to other similar firms also. And when you add on top of this some of management's rhetoric, such as how current domestic policy in the US is helping it and how its demand environment remains robust, I can't be anything other than bullish about the firm.

data.title: Verisk Analytics Will Keep Growing Its Bottom Lines Despite Warnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

On the Seeking Alpha summary page for Verisk Analytics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VRSK), investors are warned the firm is at high risk of performing badly. Such red banner warnings might scare away potential investors, but should they in Verisk’s case?

I argue the banner and warning are the result of a short-term perspective, one that ignores the firm’s history and forward potential. So, I maintain my Buy rating.

About Verisk

At the home page of its website, the company states, “Powering Better Insurance Decisions.” It adds, “Verisk connects the global insurance industry through proprietary data and technology to deliver the insights that real people depend on every day.”

On its Investor Relations page, it states, “It empowers clients to strengthen operating efficiency, improve underwriting and claims outcomes, combat fraud, and make informed decisions about global risks, including climate change, extreme events, sustainability, and political issues. Through advanced data analytics, software, scientific research, and deep industry knowledge, Verisk helps build global resilience for individuals, communities, and businesses.”

At the close on October 22, 2025, shares traded at $237.47, and it had a market cap of $33.12 billion.

The Warning

Click on the banner, and you see this message, “Verisk Analytics, Inc. (VRSK) has characteristics that have been historically associated with poor future stock performance. VRSK is overpriced and has declining growth when compared to other Industrials stocks, to the point that it gets a Sell rating from our Quant rating system. Stocks rated Sell or worse by our Quant rating system have massively underperformed the S&P 500, as this article will describe.”

In simple terms, the Quant system behind Seeking Alpha analytics sees Verisk as overpriced and with declining growth when compared with other Industrials stocks.

Overpriced?

Let’s start with a five-year price chart:

Over the past six months, the price has dropped 17.58%; that’s a cut of nearly one-fifth.

Still, the valuation table does support the system’s assertion:

But, we have a fuller understanding of valuation after reviewing a previous table. In my January 22, 2025 article, “Institutional Investors Like Verisk Analytics, Here's Why” we see significantly higher valuation metrics.

Here are a couple of examples:

  • P/E Non-GAAP TTM: January = 43.25, October = 33.72
  • P/E GAAP TTM: January = 43.08, October = 36.11
  • EV/Sales TTM: January = 14.93, October = 11.81

As the price has fallen, its valuation has improved, but not enough to meet or beat the Industrials medians. Still, and as we will see below, VRSK has delivered above average growth and profitability, so it may not fall to fair or undervalued in the near future.

Declining Growth?

Have earnings declined? Yes, in one sense they have, but if you follow that line of reasoning, you might deceive yourself.

Here’s what happened. In the second quarter of 2024, net income and earnings shot up—on a one-time basis. Revenue was up minimally, but the bottom lines jumped noticeably. Here are the net income amounts in recent quarters:

  • Q1-2024: $219.4 million
  • Q2-2024: $308.1 million
  • Q3-2024: $220.1 million
  • Q4-2024: $210.4 million
  • Q1-2025: $232.3 million
  • Q2-2025: $253.3 million

Take the Q2-2024 line out, and you see a fairly regular upward progression, with a slight dip in Q4-2024, then solid growth in Q1-2025 and Q2-2025. We see a similar set of data for basic EPS:

  • Q1-2024: $1.53
  • Q2-2024: $2.16
  • Q3-2024: $1.55
  • Q4-2024: $1.45
  • Q1-2025: $1.66
  • Q2-2025: $1.81

Again, look at the progression if Q2-2024 is taken out; we see earnings have steadily grown.

The company reported, in its Q2-2024 earnings summary, “The increase in income from continuing operations was primarily driven by net gains of $98.3 million associated with the settlement of retained interests related to the prior sales of our healthcare business in 2016 and our specialized markets business in 2022, and a net gain of $3.6 million on the early extinguishment of debt related to a cash tender of $400.0 million aggregate principal of our 2025 Senior Notes.”

So, I would argue the Seeking Alpha system is simply wrong about declining growth. However, the full warning said declining growth “when compared to other industrial stocks.” Does that make a difference?

Not really. Here’s the Seeking Alpha growth table that compares Verisk with the Industrials medians:

In reviewing this table, scan down the “% Diff. to Sector” column; there you’ll see Verisk not only beats the medians in many cases, but also beats them by substantial margins.

Whichever way we look at the declining growth idea, we know the system is wrong and the banner should be given little or no credence.

Insiders And Institutional Investors

Investors have been nervous, bidding the price down quite dramatically. From a high of $321.33 on June 4, 2025 to $237.47 at the close on October 22. However, public shareholders own only a narrow slice of the company: 6.47%.

Insiders own an even smaller slice, 0.17%, and they’re not rushing to the exits. In the past two months, a scan of the Seeking Alpha insider transactions shows a few executives and an independent director selling shares. However, they’ve been selling in lots of a few hundred or a few thousand, so it appears they’re just selling some of their earned stock options.

The professional investors who run institutional investing firms are also maintaining their positions, overall. When I analyzed Verisk on January 2025, they held 92.5% of the outstanding shares. Ten months later they own 92.94%, a modest increase, but an increase nevertheless.

So, insiders and professional investors are apparently expecting Verisk to keep growing profitably.

Profitability

Speaking of profitability, Verisk handily outperforms the Industrials medians:

Ignore the return on common equity; that reflects a significant drop in common equity on the balance sheet. It peaked at $2.842 billion in 2021; in 2024, it was down to just $100.1 million. That’s because the firm has been actively buying back shares, which removes cash (an asset) and increases the treasury stock, and total equity goes down.

Compare these two situations. At the end of 2015, total equity was $1.372 billion and net income was $507.6 million; using the formula of net income divided by total equity, that works out to 37%.

At the end of 2024, total equity is so low, at $100.1 million, and net income is at $958.2 million, that the formula produces an unrealistic result.

A more reasonable estimate of profitability might come from Return on Total Capital, which clocks in at 22.14%, earning a grade of A+.

What Is The Outlook?

The following slide from the Q2-2025 earnings presentation shows what Verisk management expects for full-year 2025:

Next, compare the key fundamentals in that slide with full-year 2024:

  • Revenue: $2.882 billion
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $1.576 billion
  • Diluted Adjusted EPS: $6.64

In its guidance, Verisk management sees modest growth in revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and diluted adjusted EPS this year. All respectable gains.

To preview Q3 results, expected on October 29, we will compare year-to-date results with the firm’s guidance. Then, we will divide the differences in two, because two quarterly reports remain for this year:

The first column contains management guidance for the full year 2025; the second shows actual results for the first half; the third shows the difference between columns one and two; the fourth shows a Q3 estimate by dividing column 3 in two; column five shows actual Q3-2024 results; column six shows the dollar difference between estimated Q3-2025 and actual Q3-2024; and column seven shows the percentage difference.

in other words, I expect Q3-2025 revenue of $797.0 million, a 9.93% increase over the same period last year. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to increase 7.11%, and diluted adjusted EPS is expected to grow by 4.19%.

Looking ahead several years, the Wall Street analysts are positive:

The analysts also expect productivity gains beginning in 2026, where they see EPS growth exceeding revenue growth:

Verisk’s Tailwinds

It has made two major acquisitions so far this year, and both are expected to contribute immediately. CFO Elizabeth D. Mann said on the Q2 earnings call, “We are expecting revenue contribution in the range of $40 million to $50 million from all acquisitions this year, assuming that the AccuLynx transaction closes at the end of the third quarter of 2025. And we expect the transaction to be accretive to earnings by year-end 2026.”

And, it should see organic growth as well. For example, Verisk plans to add a new arrow to its quiver this year; CEO Lee Shavel said on the earnings call, “we are in the early stages of a commercial introduction of Underwriting Assistant, our AI-powered solution that produces transformative underwriting results with greater efficiency and data accuracy, enabling users to maximize their productivity and effectiveness.”

Valuation And Price Targets

As we saw above, valuation metrics are far better now than they were in January, mainly because the share price has fallen. The stock is still pricey, but moving in the right direction for buyers (for sellers, not so much).

To determine a one-year price target, I will average the estimated earnings growth for 2025 and 2026. Two quarters remain in 2025; two more quarters (2026) lie ahead within the next year.

Wall Street analysts expect 2025 EPS to rise 5.27%, year-over-year, and an increase of 11.54% in 2026. The average works out 8.41%.

Adding that amount to the closing price on October 22, $237.47, gives us a target of $257.44.

Based on this target price, the irrelevance of the system banner/warning, growth, profitability, and other factors, I maintain my previous Buy rating.

The Wall Street analysts are much more bullish, with an average one-year target price of $290.53, which is a 22.34% increase. Their low estimate is $220.00 and their high estimate is $334.00.

They have a collective Buy rating, based on six Strong Buys, three Buys, nine Holds, and one Strong Sell. Despite these numbers, they have 12 Down revisions and one up revision.

The Quant system has a Strong Sell rating, which is consistent with the red banner and warning we saw above. There has been one other Seeking Alpha analyst rating in the past 90 days, a Sell.

Risk Factors

The system may be right, and I may be wrong about its warnings and Strong Sell ratings. If that is the case, the stock price may continue to fall, rather than pick up as I expect.

A commentator on my previous article, bluescorpion0, wondered if Verisk’s business might be challenged by insurers moving their AI modeling in-house. I don’t consider this a serious threat, but it is one investors should be aware of.

The company calls its markets highly competitive. That’s due in part to there being few barriers to entry, so investors should note that competitive threats come from not only other big analytics firms, but also from small tech companies with disruptive ideas and products.

Much of its business (70% in 2024) comes from American property & casualty insurers, so a downturn or soft markets could reduce its growth opportunities. Threats to insurers include loss experiences and capital capacity.

As a technology company, Verisk is exposed to cybersecurity risks. These might affect the reliability of its data, the effectiveness of its solutions, or involve privacy breaches.

Conclusion

I believe the Quant and system warnings of Verisk Analytics being overpriced and subject to declining growth are incorrect. Regarding overpricing, we see overvaluation, but then again, companies that enjoy this type of growth and profitability are likely to be expensive. On declining growth, the warning was triggered by a one-off gain in the second quarter of 2024.

Otherwise, Verisk appears to be growing reasonably well, especially in 2026 and 2027, when it is expected to produce double-digit earnings increases.

Therefore, I maintain my buy rating.

data.title: Palantir Just Plugged Into Lumen's Network, Here's Why It Matters
data.description:
Thesis

The news is out: Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN) has just signed a partnership extension with Palantir (PLTR), tipped to be valued at $200 million. It's a deal that should see Palantir's AI infrastructure get incorporated into Lumen's already existing and pretty extensive fibre network. I see several benefits from this. First, Lumen itself will get access to Palantir's AI internally. I'm expecting them to use this to optimize their own operations, which should lead to more cost savings. Secondly, Lumen now gets to offer their own fibre network, as they have Palantir's infrastructure already incorporated, an aspect that should make their services more attractive to enterprise clients. I also see this partnership as having several upsides for Palantir, as I'll explain. Lumen shares are up over 10% on the back of the news. It is a stock that has recovered exceptionally well post Liberation Day. On the 6-month chart, the stock is up over 137% alongside nice AI tailwinds. Moving forward, I see more tailwinds, not just in terms of general AI, but for the deep networks that allow enterprises and hyperscalers to connect their data--connecting AI at the edge as well as connectivity for data centres. Lumen is placed well now to take advantage of this, especially with Palantir on their side.

A pivot to enterprise services

Lumen is a pretty large-scale digital infrastructure company. They mainly make money by providing the physical and the software backbone that powers anything from the internet, cloud computing, and even enterprise connectivity. Currently, they hold one of the most extensive global fibre networks across the world. Tipped to be in the hundreds of thousands of route miles of fibre for connecting data centres and cloud providers. Their revenue revolves around selling access to services built around that network. Now, this could be things like IP transit, wavelength services, Ethernet, VPNs, edge computing, or cloud connectivity. I like to think of it as if Lumen is helping big organisations move massive amounts of data between locations and cloud environments. And this is where the bulk of their revenue comes from, via enterprise and wholesale customers who need their data moved. So think hyperscalers, governments, financial institutions, and large corporations that want capacity and bandwidth. Lumen also operates something called Quantum Fibre, which provides fibre-based internet to residential and small businesses. However, in recent times, it seems they’ve been pivoting away from mass-market consumer broadband. Mainly to focus on high-margin enterprise services, which is where the money is. Their newer growth engines are, of course, in AI-driven data transport and at the edge cloud hosting. So processing AI tasks at the site of data collection, without the need to transfer data back to a centre.

A deal with Palantir

As you all know, the biggest news to come from Lumen was a new $200 million expansion with Palantir. Now, I see this as more than just a vendor contract, it’s a pretty layered, strategic partnership where Palantir get their data intelligence stack to be fused into Lumen’s physical network infrastructure. It’s being structured as a multi-year commitment from both sides, with Lumen tipped to spend roughly $200 million on Palantir’s software/services, but spread over several years. However, we should view it more as an ecosystem deal. You see, Palantir isn’t just selling licenses here; it wants to embed its Foundry and Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP) directly into Lumen’s connectivity network. So they want it built into the way Lumen’s network actually operates and delivers services. Foundry is essentially Palantir’s data integration and analytics engine. So it can pull together information from thousands of sources, then clean it, and later make it usable for decision-making. AIP then layers in that advanced AI part on top of that data. So anything from using models to automate actions, predict outcomes, and optimise performance in real time. On the flip side, Lumen’s Connectivity Fabric role is a high-performance network architecture to connect the data centres or cloud regions to enterprise sites. Basically, the infrastructure that moves petabytes of information globally.

The immediate benefit I see for Lumen from this expansion is first they get to use Palantir’s AI internally. They can now optimise their own network operations. This might include automating provisioning, predicting outages, and of course, streamlining their massive data flows. In fact, Lumen has already reported that they were able to save hundreds of millions in annualised cost just from using Palantir’s tech. Second, and more strategically, Lumen now gets to turn around and just resell this combined AI-network product to enterprise clients. I expected to market it as a much-improved, better product offering. Something that basically says: instead of buying network capacity from Lumen and then adding your own AI tools, you can now buy AI-ready infrastructure to handle all that data movement and intelligence together. Their enterprise clients will view it as faster and more secure.

What Palantir are getting out of this

If we look at it from Palantir’s side, this should be viewed as a major commercial victory for them. It’s a nice way to move more towards a smart scaling play. The $200 million headline will give them a guaranteed revenue stream and high-margin software deal, but the deeper prize they want from this is the distribution they can get out of Lumen’s network. If you dig a bit into Lumen’s enterprise customer base, you’ll see that it is massive. They have global banks, manufacturers, huge logistics companies, and even some pretty big energy providers using their network. All of whom are already paying for the bandwidth, connectivity, and edge computing aspects of Lumen's offerings. So by Palantir plugging their Foundry/AIP into that same pipeline, they get a new go-to-market channel without building it themselves. The network was essentially already there, waiting for them. Lumen did the hard work of building it, and now Palantir get to tap into it for a price. It’s a pretty powerful synergy when you think about it.

I also see the partnership giving a little bit of help for Palantir when it comes to pushing into commercial infrastructure--somewhere between the traditional government contracts we’ve been seeing and no new hyperscale cloud opportunities. The more we see its software become the brain behind network operations, the harder it becomes for enterprises to switch away down the line. For Palantir investors, we want to see this long-tail of sticky, recurring revenue, and for Lumen investors, the fact that they now have a Palantir offering should land them more customers.

The way I see this deal going is that a big tailwind from the AI bubble is just about building the fastest or most advanced model. A big part of it is going to be about who can move big data quickly and securely between the cloud, the edge, and then the on-prem systems. This is exactly the space where Lumen operates. Palantir should now also get that positioning of its platforms into a real-time data distribution for AI at network speed. If you think about it from a hyperscaler's perspective, players like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud can’t actually fully replicate that. They’d need proximity to the physical layer where the data actually travels, such as Lumen's network. Now, for Lumen, the deal is much simpler; they are set to gain a massive credibility boost just from embedding a top-tier AI platform into their infrastructure. So while that $200 million extension figure seems pretty big, the real value here down the line is from control over how enterprise AI is deployed at scale. Palantir will get the brain, Lumen will get the backbone along which signals will travel, and together they’re essentially a nervous system for enterprise networks that need AI.

Valuation

Lumen’s current valuation picture seems like it’s still pretty discounted relative to its sector. However, over the past day, it looks like it’s beginning to rerate as the market starts to price in some operational outcome and new client contracts from the deal. They hold a forward EV to sales ratio of 1.93x, just trading slightly below the sector median of 1.97x. So it’s valued modestly lower than its peers despite the improving fundamentals we’re seeing with the growing exposure to AI-driven infrastructure. Even better is its forward price-to-sales ratio of just 0.58x, against a sector median of 1.27x. That's quite a steep discount at 54%, which suggests the market is still seeing Lumen as a laggard telecom rather than an emerging digital infrastructure play with a bit of risk. Yet, that gap is exactly what gives me a positive bias from a valuation point of view. Lumen is trading at deep-value multiples, while its operational metrics, particularly cash flow and margin improvements, are showing some momentum. Over the trailing twelve months, the company managed to generate roughly $1.6 billion in operating cash flow, which comes to a price-to-cash-flow ratio of just 1.65x and a forward ratio of 1.34x. That’s compared to the sector’s 8.40x, so a hefty 84% discount. On the profitability side of things, Lumen’s forward EV to EBITDA sits at 7.06x, versus 8.39x for the sector, so some margin recovery here despite legacy business headwinds. Those improvements would come mainly from operational streamlining. We saw those cost reductions exceeding $350 million annually.

Areas of concern

Now, even with the renewed optimism from this extension, I see Lumen still having a few material risks that we have to touch upon. The biggest being the fact that we need to see Lumen turning this AI-driven narrative into sustainable, profitable growth. The Palantir partnership obviously should help out with that, but integrating complex AI systems into a legacy network is operationally very demanding and could take longer, and of course, cost more than expected. Debt load remains another key concern in line with this. They hold roughly $18 billion in total debt against just $1.6 billion in cash, so balance-sheet flexibility is quite limited. There’s also customer churn, which we often see within legacy services. That’s mainly due to the scale of competition we see. I’m expecting Lumen to face pressure from hyperscalers and other fibre operators. So, in short, while the upside case seems clear, the turnaround still depends heavily on flawless execution with that limited cash position.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, things get pretty interesting for Lumen. The next big news update we should get will be 3Q25 earnings. It should come sometime around October 30th, after market close. That will be the first real checkpoint where we’d want to see some evidence that the Palantir deal, among other AI-related investments, may translate into growth. So what should investors be watching for? First, any signs of new contract bookings with AI, edge, and enterprise clients. Especially revenue linked to the embedded Foundry/AIP and Connectivity Fabric combo I just explained. If Lumen can show that the pipeline they are building is more attractive for enterprise clients, it should turn into real recurring revenue, which is validation that the pivot is working. Second, I’d like to see margin expansion and EBITDA growth to be evident. Wall Street is projecting revenue to come in at around $3.09 billion and EPS below breakeven, so -$0.24. There's some cautious optimism here after several soft quarters that we saw. With this deal, it's no longer enough just to cite cost cuts; profits must move in the right direction now, even in legacy lines.

data.title: Rising Japanese Inflation Keeps Door Open For A December Rate Hike
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Consumer prices rose to 2.9% YoY in September from 2.7%

Japan’s consumer price inflation rose to 2.9% year-on-year in September from the previous month’s 2.7% rate, in line with market consensus. The largest gain was in utilities, which rose 1.9% from the previous -4.0%, related to the government energy subsidy program. Underlying inflationary pressure is still firm. Other data indicate that firm wage growth is boosting private spending. In addition, the weak JPY is likely to add more pressure in the near term. Thus, this will support the BoJ’s rate hike in the coming months.

Inflation to stay above 2% for an extended period, supporting gradual BoJ rate hikes

BoJ watch

The timing of the next rate hike remains uncertain. There are differing opinions, as indicated by two dissenting votes in the previous meeting. This situation is expected to continue at the 29-30 October meeting. It is anticipated that there will be two dissenting votes without significantly affecting the overall outcome. Most board members are cautious about the recent re-escalation of trade tensions between the US and China and current US economic conditions. Therefore, the BoJ is likely to continue its rate-hike pause in October. Thus, we now see a December hike as our base case scenario.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Valmont Industries: Power Secular Tailwinds With A Strong Engineering Moat
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Valmont Industries (NYSE:VMI) is a buy rating. I believe VMI is very well-positioned to capitalize on the long-term growth from secular tailwinds. Its infrastructure segment directly benefits from grid modernization, renewable integration, and 5G buildout, while the agriculture segment addresses water scarcity. Its ability to capitalize on this tailwind lies in its engineering moat, one that is hard to replicate easily.

Business Overview

VMI has two segments. The Infrastructure segment, which makes up ~73% of sales, produces products like utility poles, lighting and traffic structures, and telecommunications towers and provides protective coating services. The Agriculture segment makes mechanized irrigation systems under the well-known Valley brand.

Riding Powerful Secular Tailwinds

There are powerful secular tailwinds that are driving VMI's growth. In the infrastructure segment, I believe VMI is a direct beneficiary of the ongoing multi-decade investment cycle in electrification (i.e., internal combustion engine [ICE] to electric vehicles [EVs]), grid modernization (to incorporate renewable energy), and the energy transition (fossil fuel to gas or renewables). And the urgency is real. Utilities are rushing to upgrade the aging US grid infrastructure to handle growing electricity demand from data centers and EVs and integrate renewable power generation.

This is necessary capex spending that must be spent in order to support the broader demand needs (AI, EVs, etc.), and as such, this effectively detaches VMI from the end-demand cyclicality. Basically, VMI growth is tied to non-discretionary spending, and this gives a lot of comfort in the growth outlook. The scale of this demand opportunity is incredible. In the recent Q2 earnings call, management cited a Jefferies report that forecasted US utility capex to go beyond $212 billion in 2025, which implies a solid 22% y/y growth. While the main aspect of this overhaul is the grid system itself, you cannot do it without the transmission poles, distribution structures, and substation components. As you can guess, these are products that VMI produces, and hence, I think VMI is in a solid position to ride on this capex cycle.

Aside from utilities, the rollout of 5G networks and increasing connectivity requirements also drive solid demand for VMI's telecommunications towers and related components. Think of the difference between 4G and 5G. 5G technology operates on higher-frequency radio waves that can carry more data at faster speeds, but the downside is they travel shorter distances and cannot penetrate buildings well. To make up for this shortcoming, carriers can't simply upgrade existing towers; they must build a larger network of cell sites placed closer together. You would know this process as densification.

For VMI, they supply the physical infrastructure to enable this buildout-things like macro towers for wide coverage and mounting components that secure antennas to these structures. In essence, the more sites carriers construct to support 5G, the more towers, poles, and hardware VMI sells. You can see how strong the demand is just by looking at the recent quarter, where telecom sales grew >40% y/y.

As for the agriculture segment, the outlook is equally compelling. As the world's population grows, there is an increasing need to produce more food with fewer resources. There are many inputs that go into agricultural production, and one of the most important inputs is water, especially in regions with lesser/infrequent rainfall. Traditional farming methods, such as flood irrigation, are just not efficient at all. It's like watering a garden with a fire hose; you get some areas overwatered, some left dry, and the water runs off before reaching the roots. The result is uneven growth, lower yields, and massive waste.

VMI's valley irrigation systems are game-changing technology for farmers. Basically, it includes large, rotating structures that deliver water evenly across wide fields through sprinklers. The concept is simple: by distributing water where and when it's needed, it leads to improved yields and water conservation. On the yield side, uniform distribution of water ensures that each plant receives the right amount of water for healthy growth. There wouldn't be any overwatered or dry spots, and that leads to more consistent and predictable output. For water conservation, it works in a sense that VMI's system will calculate how much water is needed, and it delivers just the right amount. So instead of VMI "recollecting" water, it makes sure farmers can use water efficiently.

These two dynamics should continue to drive farmers to adopt VMI's product, especially as drought conditions intensify and the cost of water increases.

Competitive Moat in the Infrastructure Segment

Having strong secular tailwinds is great, but VMI needs to be able to capitalize on it. This is where VMI's moat becomes important to understand. In the infrastructure segment, while the products VMI's make might sound like a commodity, they are not. The products are mission critical, and hence the product must work reliably. While a standard steel pole may corrode within a few years, VMI's hot-dip galvanized structures are designed to last over a century. This is an incredibly important consideration for utilities because they are making multi-decade investments in grid infrastructure. The longer these structures last, the lower the total cost of ownership and return on investment, as less maintenance capex is required.

If you think creating these structures at this scale is simple, you are wrong. This is far from simple, and the complexity of this is the core of VMI's moat. For hot-dip galvanized structures, one would need a very precise industrial process. Just for an overview, the process involves submerging fully fabricated steel structures (sometimes as large as a 250-foot transmission pole) into molten zinc heated to ~450 degrees. This would give the pole a coating that prevents corrosion. Replicating this capability at scale is extremely difficult for two reasons.

Number one, the initial capex is high. Just to give you a better perspective, a galvanizing kettle large enough for utility poles is the size of a swimming pool and holds hundreds of tons of molten zinc. That is just the kettle itself. You would also need a logistical network to lift, drop, and move these poles, which means you need a lot of cranes and equipment required to maneuver the poles. These are huge capital requirements and take time to build.

Number two, say that you have the financial power; it would also take years to master the process. Remember, you need precise industrial steps, from surface cleaning to immersion time and cooling. In this aspect, VMI has ~80 years of accumulated know-how.

Recap on Q3 2025 earnings

Q3 performance was good. Net sales grew 2.5% y/y to ~$1.05 billion, which may seem soft on the surface, but I think it is actually solid. The Infrastructure segment saw sales up 6.6% y/y, driven by double-digit growth in Utility (12.3%) and Telecommunications (37%). The Coatings business also saw a healthy 9.7% y/y growth. And perhaps the most encouraging indicator that gives conviction is that this demand is translating into visibility. VMI now has backlog that is up >20% since end of FY2024. Agriculture was the weak segment, where sales fell 9% y/y. North American demand remained muted as farmers continued to stay cautious on investments, and international sales were also not great due to project timing in the Middle East vs. last year.

Valuation

While VMI's multiple (forward PE) has already inflected upwards, now at 20x (the +1 standard deviation level), I wouldn't look at this and dismiss the potential for further re-rating. For what it's worth, this entire re-rating was not just isolated to VMI but for peers as well. The chart above shows that since April, the peer average multiple has also risen in the same magnitude. And I think this reflects the market's positive view on the industry's exposure to the tailwinds I mentioned above. In other words, I believe this re-rating is structural, and historical multiples are no longer a good benchmark. If VMI can trade up to 20x, who is to say it cannot trade up to 25x-peaking in early 2021?

A fast way to illustrate the upside is to value it on what the consensus is expecting (which has been quite accurate anyway). Take FY28 adj. EPS of $26, for instance; suppose multiples stay at the current 20x, this implies a share price target of $520, and should multiples go higher-which could happen if we go into an AI bubble mode-at 25x, the stock could be worth more.

Risk

One of the key risks here is VMI's exposure to volatility in steel and zinc prices. These are important raw inputs, and should prices go up, it could compress margins. While the utility business benefits from a strong visible backlog, other parts of the portfolio, like Lighting & Transportation and Coatings, have greater exposure to cyclical construction and industrial activity. Depending on the macro conditions, these exposures could drag down overall performance.

Conclusion

I recommend a buy rating for VMI, as this is a business with solid exposure to structural tailwinds and has a competitive moat. While raw material volatility remains a near-term risk, I think the overall growth tailwind should more than overcome these cost pressures as underlying demand is strong. Looking ahead, I believe the path for earnings to grow is clear, and so long as nothing major happens to the macroeconomy (like a pandemic or major natural disaster), VMI earnings should compound nicely and could see further multiple re-rating.

data.title: Novo Nordisk's Lottery Ticket Against Alzheimer's Disease
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NVO) will be presenting results from its phase 3 trials for semaglutide for early Alzheimer’s disease at the Clinical Trials on Alzheimer’s Disease conference in early December. Even company executives have different forecasts for their drug candidate’s chances of success, ranging from high hopes to comparing it to a lottery ticket. As explained below, semaglutide probably will have relatively minimal effects on early Alzheimer's disease. Whatever the results, Novo Nordisk’s stock value is more likely to be affected by several other factors.

Some have described Alzheimer’s disease as type three diabetes. If this were the case, semaglutide might be the key to preventing Alzheimer’s disease and to significantly slowing down its early progression (optimistic prediction). Diabetes, however, is only one of many risk factors for Alzheimer’s disease. As such, semaglutide is likely to have little effect on non-diabetics (although it may also help those with prediabetes).

Having said that, diabetes is still a major risk factor for Alzheimer's disease. To the extent that semaglutide (found in Ozempic and Wegovy) limits glucose levels in the brain, it may substantially reduce the risk for Alzheimer’s disease for a large group of people (although the same could also be said about a strict adherence to a Mediterranean diet).

One of the effects of semaglutide is to increase the transport and metabolism of glucose in various parts of the body. This may not be as true for the brain, however, where nitration impedes the transport of glucose. This may be why in Novo Nordisk's phase 2 study of liraglutide - - another GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) inhibitor - - the primary endpoint of improved glucose metabolism in the brain was not met (trial results). Furthermore, impaired glucose transport and metabolism is only one of several factors that impair cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease.

Semaglutide, though, might improve cognitive function in other ways. In theory, at least, reducing blood glucose levels in other parts of the body would reduce glucose-6 phosphate and myo-inositol levels in the brain (see chart below). This is important because high levels of myo-inositol are sometimes a precursor for Alzheimer's disease.

Other factors that increase myo-inositol levels in the brain include high blood pressure due to high sodium levels and Down syndrome (because individuals with Down syndrome have an extra copy of the sodium/myo-inositol cotransporter gene). Of the various means of inhibiting myo-inositol, low dose lithium may be the most effective and safest. In any case, the combination of GLP-1 agonists, certain high blood pressure medications, and low dose lithium may substantially lower the risk for Alzheimer’s disease and slow its early progression.

Semaglutide may also affect some of the early stimuli for Alzheimer's disease. As shown below, these early stimuli often work by over-activating receptor tyrosine kinase or g protein-coupled receptors.

Here the effects of GLP-1 agonists may be a mixed bag. They may reduce the over-activation of the platelet derived growth factor receptor (a receptor tyrosine kinase) by glucose, but the GLP-1 receptor is a g protein-coupled receptor. Perhaps, this is why liraglutide only reduced the progression of mild Alzheimer's disease by 18 percent compared to placebo. The semaglutide trials may produce somewhat better results but they are not likely to be transformational.

Potentially, semaglutide may lower the risk for Alzheimer’s disease and slow its early progression in those with prediabetes and diabetes. With long-term use, though, some of the more severe effects of GLP-1 agonists may become more common, such as vision loss, bowel obstruction, and pancreatitis. Unless the results are unequivocally good, the risk-benefit analysis is not clear cut. In any case, the attempt to extend treatment to new conditions may not extend the market that much. A person, for example, is not likely to say I don’t want to take the drug for my diabetes, but I do want to take it to reduce my future risk for Alzheimer’s disease (if that is what additional trials show). Furthermore, any expanded market might have to be shared with rivals such as Eli Lilly (LLY), who are likely to conduct their own trials. On the other hand, with expectations already tempered, even poor results are not likely to drag Novo Nordisk’s stock down too far.

There are other factors that may affect Novo Nordisk’s stock value. Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly both face potential price controls in the United States, although most investors probably doubt that will come to pass.

Novo Nordisk has faced internal problems, including turnovers in leadership (news story). In addition, up to this point Eli Lilly seems to have the upper hand over Novo Nordisk in the GLP-1 marathon. In the short-term at least, the floor and the ceiling for Novo Nordisk do not seem far apart. Therefore, my hold recommendation, both considering and excluding Novo Nordisk’s upcoming Alzheimer’s trial results.

data.title: The Race To Break China's Rare Earth Supply Control
data.description:
Rare earths have become a strategic lever in global trade. As China tightens export controls, nations are racing to build domestic supply chains and secure access to these critical materials.

Transcript

So rare earth and strategic metals have become a powerful bargaining chip in global trade. China, which dominates global supply, recently tightened export restrictions on key rare earth metals, which is really a big deal since these materials power everything from your phone to electric vehicles and fighter jets. And that move has set off a new wave of urgency worldwide as countries are scrambling to kind of rebuild their own supply chains and cut dependence on China.

And the past few months, markets have really reflected that tension. Prices for magnet materials like neodymium remain up nearly 40% year on year. Tungsten has nearly doubled on tighter supply. But the bigger story isn't price though, really, it's policy. China's expanded export controls and the US threat of tariffs up to 100% have really kind of reignited this race to secure non-Chinese supply chains.

And in the US and abroad, that effort is gaining traction. The Pentagon is now the largest shareholder in MP Materials (MP), anchoring this private-public partnership that sort of guarantees longer-term pricing and magnet offtake. And other companies are also advancing with the Department of Energy and Defense and funding to scale domestic production of critical materials.

So while the fundamentals are mixed across metals, investment momentum is really building fast. Rare earths aren't just a niche material story anymore. We believe they've become a strategic lever in global trade and really a compelling opportunity for investors that are watching this space.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Veeva Systems: A Great Company At The Wrong Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As a conservative investor, I treat Veeva Systems (NYSE:VEEV) in two ways: I like the business itself, the company has excellent financial performance, high growth rates, and there is no debt, it would seem, take it and buy. But the stumbling block in this story is the unreasonable valuation – Veeva is trading too expensive, I would say obscenely expensive. And I am inclined to believe that investors are too positive about the company and do not take into account the fact that such business growth rates will not last forever – in the near future, the company will move to maturity and the growth rate will gradually slow down. This may disappoint investors, although it is a natural development process for any company. At the moment, I don't see any strong growth factors justifying the purchase of Veeva at this price. But I also don't see any significant reasons for shareholders to sell them right now. In such a situation, the most logical thing would be a Hold rating and a recommendation for those interested in Veeva to buy shares with price corrections below $220. Later in the text, I will substantiate the thesis I have put forward.

A Strong Franchise Facing Growth Fatigue

In a nutshell, Veeva is engaged in the supply of software for companies in the field of Life Sciences. Accordingly, the clients are pharmaceutical companies, biotech, medtech and other startups in the field of medicine. According to the company, it already has more than 1,000 customers, and 19 of the world's 20 largest pharmaceutical companies are serviced by Veeva. Veeva's business model focuses on software based on the SaaS model: key products cover the entire cycle of drug development and market launch. By purchasing Veeva software, customers can conveniently store and manage documents (Veeva Vault), maintain a CRM system (Veeva CRM), track data on customers, doctors, hospitals, clinical trials (Veeva Network), and analyze marketing and sales effectiveness. Having set up all these systems at home, pharmaceutical companies are reluctant to abandon them later, as it will be expensive and time-consuming, and therefore Veeva has almost no customer outflow.

From recent developments, I would mention the development of the Vault platform, to which management plans to transfer its CRM product by 2030, abandoning Salesforce. Development is in full swing, and this is being done in order to reduce dependence on other companies. In August 2025, Veeva also announced a partnership with IQVIA (a major pharma data provider), with which there had previously been legal disputes. All legal proceedings have now been settled, and cooperation will make it easier for customers to combine Veeva products with IQVIA services. Well, I follow the trends, Veeva is obviously actively investing in AI. The first AI modules for CRM are planned to be released in December, and in 2026 for clinical, regulatory, and other applications. It is clear that management is looking for new growth points with all its might, as the old ones are gradually fading away.

Another Solid Quarter, Yet Early Signs of Maturity

Let's move on to the urgent – the analysis of Veeva's latest quarterly results. According to the company's financial calendar, the third quarter of 2026 is now underway, and accordingly I will analyze the report for the second quarter of 2026.

To begin with, the report as a whole was within expectations, without disappointing or greatly encouraging. Revenue increased by 17% YoY to $789.1 million, which was in line with Wall Street expectations.

Yes, the growth rate is good, but there are also nuances. For example, normalized billings grew by only +10% YoY in Q2, which is slightly lower than the revenue rate. This may signal clients' caution in entering into large new contracts. In general, normalized billings is, of course, quite volatile and jumps from quarter to quarter, but its slowdown confirms that Veeva is entering a more mature growth phase. Therefore, you need to be careful here and carefully monitor the dynamics of total revenue and segments separately, since at the first signs of a steady slowdown, stocks are likely to adjust strongly, since right now their value is so high that it feels like investors expect such growth rates forever.

Operating profit and net profit also increased by 18% and 17%, respectively, according to GAAP. Non-GAAP operating profit increased by 26% and represents about 44.7% margin. The operating margin was 24.8%, and the net profit margin was 25%. EPS was $1.19 (versus $1.04 a year earlier), and adjusted EPS was $1.99 versus $1.62 a year ago, beating consensus by a few cents. The company has no problems with profitability, at the moment there is nothing pressing on marginality, and the company continues to increase it.

The quarterly results confirmed Veeva's ability to maintain high profitability even with slowing revenue growth. Management noted that the results exceeded their own forecast in all indicators: revenue came out slightly above the upper limit of guidance, and profit exceeded expectations due to faster subscription growth. Next, let's look at what management expects from the third quarter and I'll give you my forecasts.

Management Stays Conservative as Growth Settles

Guidance is quite restrained by the company, both throughout the year and in the coming quarter, but it is not far from Wall Street's expectations. Especially with regard to the upper limit of forecasts. The company expects total revenue in the range of $3.13-3.13 billion, while the same consensus forecasts $3.13-3.17 billion. The same goes for EPS: management expects $7.78, while the market expects about $7.81. This also applies to forecasts for the third quarter. According to most indicators, Wall Street expects closer to the upper limit of the management forecast. What is important is that the company expects Normalized Billings to grow by only 13%, which is obviously a continuation of the slowing trend. As I said, this could be an indicator of a future revenue slowdown.

My opinion generally coincides with guidance. I expect revenue for the third quarter of about $793 million due to the traditional growth in customer activity in the fall. Adjusted profitability will remain high – about 44% – which will give EPS in the region of $1.95. In annual terms, this corresponds to 13-14% revenue growth and 12% profit growth, a slowdown from 16-17% in the first half of the year. In my opinion and in the opinion of management (given their guidance), the company currently has no obvious drivers for acceleration, and the company will gradually move towards normalization of growth rates.

Valuation Stretch Becoming Hard to Ignore

Let's move on to the main thing, namely the assessment. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap, with EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value, from which Net Debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

When calculating the unlevered FCF 2026-2030, I included several prerequisites. Firstly, in my model, the revenue growth rate will decrease, and the CAGR will be about 11.26%. I explain this by the fact that the company is gradually entering the stage of maturity and a slowdown in growth is inevitable, and Normalized billings is already signaling this. The average operating margin for the period will be about 25-28%. Here I have laid down growth, but conservative, since the company, becoming mature, will gradually sacrifice growth rates for the sake of marginality. Veeva's CapEx has historically been low, which is logical and related to the specifics of its activities – in my model it will remain in the range of 0.7-0.8%, which is slightly higher than the historical average. Changes in working capital will be mostly negative due to customer down payments and steady growth in deferred revenue. As a result, the average FCF yield will be about 21.43%. For comparison, in the period 2020-2025, the indicator was 22.56%. This suggests that I have kept a little conservative in my forecasts, but I have not overreacted with it either.

I got the WACC for Veeva at 8.27%, which is primarily a near-zero cost of debt, since the company does not have a debt burden and because of the five-year beta at 0.96x (the remaining prerequisites are listed in the table above).

The table above vividly illustrates that the company is highly overvalued by multiples compared to the sectoral averages. Yes, these multiples are high for a reason – indeed, they reflect Veeva's uniqueness and dominance in its segment, which obviously requires some premium to the market. But the 100-200% premium is already starting to embarrass. I have already said that the market evaluates Veeva as if it will grow so fast forever. To justify such an assessment, we must see in the financial statements how Veeva consistently exceeds forecasts or accelerates. But there are no signs of this yet. Therefore, I tend to consider stocks overvalued at the moment, and the results of the DCF model also demonstrate this.

Both methods of calculating terminal value (using P/E and EV/EBITDA) give or take the same result. In the basic scenario of both methods, the downside is about 21%. In the worst case scenario, downside ranges from 22-26%, and in the best case, from 15% to 19.7%. Obviously, the model confirms the obvious revaluation of Veeva shares. Based on the results of the DCF model, I would recommend that investors buy stocks with a price correction of more than 20%, that is, closer to $220.

Selective Buybacks Reflect Valuation Caution

Veeva is a typical growth stock with high rates of revenue growth and operational performance, which invests in growth, has no debt burden, and does not pay dividends. But she still started holding buybacks in 2024. A $500 million buyback program was approved, and during FY2025, the company began to buy back shares on the open market (in the FY2025 financial statements, a decrease in the cash for repurchasing its own shares was recorded). Nevertheless, a significant amount of authorization, in my opinion, still remains unrealized – management is in no hurry to aggressively buy shares at current prices. They probably view buyback more as a tool to compensate for dilution from option programs and as an option in case of a significant drawdown in quotations. This may tell us that they themselves consider their shares to be too overbought right now.

Risks

First of all, I would consider the risk of reducing revenue growth. Once this process begins, stocks can adjust strongly. Revenue will stop growing so fast when large pharmaceutical companies fully satisfy their needs for new modules, and smaller ones begin to reduce their IT budgets due to macroeconomic difficulties, for example. Competition will grow gradually, and therefore Veeva's management must always remain on the alert, new players can dump prices in a high-margin business and take away market share. As a whole, Veeva is completely dependent on the pharmaceutical market, and if problems suddenly arise there (with regulation, for example, or macroeconomic ones), it will undoubtedly hit the business.

Final Thoughts

Veeva is a great business, and I would be very happy to recommend buying shares if it weren't for the ultra-high valuation. Veeva is not just trading at a premium, it is really overvalued. It is clear that the company is expected to have a great future, but with such an assessment, it must prove its great future in every financial report, but this does not happen. And the first signs of a slowdown are already visible. I assign a Hold rating to the company and recommend that those interested in Veeva buy shares with price corrections below $220. I don't see any strong factors for shareholders to sell them right now either.

data.title: SCHG: A 'Set-And-Forget' AI Play
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHG) is set to benefit from a multi-trillion ramp in AI-driven data center upgrades that could lead to serious excess returns compared to the market in the next several years. The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF is a compelling bet for investors that seek a ‘set-and-forget’ solution to their investment needs and want to play a multi-trillion opportunity in the technology sector: artificial intelligence. Further, with the large-cap U.S. tech sector set for a strong earnings season in Q3 '25, I believe the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF could be an investment that significantly outperforms the broader stock market, and it could be set to reach new all-time highs as well.

Previous rating

I rated SCHG a strong buy in July 2025, mainly due to hyperscalers increasing their CapEx spend, which ultimately led to new all-time highs for some of SCHG’s core holdings, including NVIDIA (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META). SCHG is a concentrated bet on continuing CapEx spending trends in the Data Center market as well as a major outperformance candidate if Magnificent 7 companies reach new record market caps during the Q3 earnings season.

Strong CapEx Growth Could Drive Tech Companies To Record Market Caps

According to Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang, the Data Center market is set to soar to a $3-4 trillion annual spending level by the end of the decade, driven by accelerating adoption of artificial intelligence. The key growth theme for SCHG and other large-cap ETFs weighted towards the technology sector is AI integration across businesses. With Magnificent 7 companies leading AI investments, there is an opportunity for investors to benefit from a diversified ETF solution like SCHG.

Recent deals in the Data Center market — which for instance, include OpenAI (OPENAI)'s $100B investment partnership with Nvidia — indicate to me that investors are still underestimating the speed with which this buildout is progressing.

OpenAI, one of the most aggressive investors in AI infrastructure, has guided for $850B in targeted Data Center investments and has struck multi-billion dollar deals with companies other than Nvidia, including AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO) and CoreWeave (CRWV)… all of which are going to provide high-performance AI chips or Cloud computing capacity for OpenAI to run its AI models.

McKinsey projects that global data center capacity demand will surge 22% on average each year until the end of the decade, with demanding AI workloads being the driving force behind the trillion dollar buildout of the global Data Center industry.

With this in mind, SCHG has the potential to deliver serious alpha for investors going forward. Nvidia is still SCHG’s largest holding, representing approximately 11.2% of the ETF’s net assets. As of October 21, 2025, SCHG managed a massive $51.4B in net assets in its portfolio. A direct competitor of SCHG is the Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG), although this ETF is significantly larger: it has total net assets of $195.3B.

According to the latest portfolio split, Apple (AAPL), which just made new all-time highs as well, replaced hyperscaler Microsoft (MSFT) as the second-largest portfolio position in the SCHG, compared to my earlier coverage of the ETF. Apple now represents 9.7% of fund investments, while Microsoft is close behind with an investment share of 9.6%.

The VUG ETF is about 3.8X larger than the SCHG, based off of assets under management, but both ETFs have strong and quite comparable investment profiles. Both ETFs overweight Nvidia, which in both cases is the respective ETF’s largest holding, and both the SCHG and VUG have ~60% of their fund assets in their top ten largest holdings.

The VUG has a slightly higher percentage (53.4%) in Magnificent 7 holdings — which include Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Amazon (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA) — but both ETFs have considerable portfolio overlap.

Strong Return Profile For Both SCHG And VUG

The outperformance of the technology market, which has been led by Nvidia in recent years, created serious alpha for ETFs that overweighted the GPU leader. SCHG, which was incepted in December 2009 (a great time to buy stocks in general), has achieved a life-to-date net asset value return of 16.8%, on average. VUG, which has been investing since January 2004, has achieved an average annual NAV return of 12.1%.

The SCHG and the VUG are growth-oriented ETFs and therefore chiefly focused on delivering net asset value growth for the benefit of investors. In the last 5-year period, the SCHG has outperformed VUG in terms of total returns: the SCHG has returned a total of 123.8% compared to a total return of 113.8% for the VUG, leading to an excess margin of 10 PP.

Risks With SCHG

ETFs that are chiefly focused on the technology sector may or may not meet investors return expectations in the future. Past performance is no guarantee of future performance, so the SCHG may not be able to repeat past investment results, especially if the tech sector rotates out of favor with investors. Additionally, the SCHG (and the VUG) have significant sector concentration risks since they explicitly seek to generate investment returns only in the U.S. large-cap technology market. A weak earnings season for the tech sector could also lead to serious valuation drawdowns for Magnificent 7 companies, especially those that have done well for investors lately, such as Nvidia, Google, Apple, Meta Platforms, and Tesla.

Closing Thoughts

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF is a very straightforward solution for investors that don’t want to go through the hassle of having to sort through individual tech companies and that want to have a diversified ETF vehicle right from the get-go. The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF has delivered highly impressive long-term returns, based off of net asset value, and I see a continuation of this strong run if the tech sector can sustain its CapEx growth. The Magnificent 7 companies are investing hundreds of billions of dollars annually into their AI products (which includes GPU and Data Center investments) which creates a long-term growth catalyst. Since the SCHG has cut its teeth over a very long period of time and has flourished regardless of the market environment, I see the ETF as a prime option for investors to beat the market and generate serious alpha.
